explicit_text: I attended grammar and junior high school near Hiroshima, and there was a girls' high school in Hiroshima City that I wished I could have gone to. My husband was originally from Hiroshima, and he had been in America before returning to Japan to marry me. Hiroshima was the backdrop of much of my early life and education before I came to America in 1917.

explicit_text: The Russo-Japanese War broke out the same year my husband took his physical examination for the draft. Because he had a crippled hand from an accident, he was exempted from military service during that war. At that time, he was going to Hawaii, and some men were called back to Japan from Hawaii due to the war. This event influenced his movements and ultimately his immigration to America.

explicit_text: I always wanted to come to America, even though I didn't know much about it before I married my husband. We came to America in 1917 after our marriage in Japan. Life here was new and different, and my husband had already been in America before. My mother was reluctant to let me go, but since he was a relative, others thought it was okay. America became my new home where I helped my family and built a life.

explicit_text: After we got our visa and passport, we went to Yokohama to catch a ship to America. The ships were crowded with picture brides and other passengers, so we had to wait in Yokohama for about a month. We stayed at a hotel during that time, which was expensive, but it was better than going home and missing another ship. Yokohama was the last place in Japan we stayed before coming to America.

explicit_text: I remember Empire's Day, which was celebrated on February 11th. On that day, we had to set up the hall for the ceremony at school. While the men did the work, a few girls, including myself, visited and talked with our teacher, who scolded us for being lazy and not helping. It was a memorable school event that showed the importance of hard work and discipline.
